self.assertEqual(event.dt, initial_dt + timedelta(hours=2))\n   594\t\n   595\t\n   596\t@override_settings(TIME_ZONE='Africa/Nairobi', USE_TZ=True)\n   597\tclass ForcedTimeZoneDatabaseTests(TransactionTestCase):\n   598\t    \"\"\"\n   599\t    Test the TIME_ZONE database configuration parameter.\n   600\t\n   601\t    Since this involves reading and writing to the same database through two\n   602\t    connections, this is a TransactionTestCase.\n   603\t    \"\"\"\n   604\t\n   605\t    available_apps = ['timezones']\n   606\t\n   607\t    @classmethod\n   608\t    def setUpClass(cls):\n   609\t        # @skipIfDBFeature and @skipUnlessDBFeature cannot be chained. The\n   610\t        # outermost takes precedence. Handle skipping manually instead.\n   611\t        if connection.features.supports_timezones:\n   612\t            raise SkipTest(\"Database has feature(s) supports_timezones\")\n   613\t        if not connection.features.test_db_allows_multiple_connections:\n   614\t            raise SkipTest(\"Database doesn't support feature(s): test_db_allows_multiple_connections\")\n   615\t\n   616\t        super().setUpClass()\n   617\t\n   618\t    def test_read_datetime(self):\n   619\t        fake_dt = datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 17, 20, 30, tzinfo=UTC)\n   620\t        Event.objects.create(dt=fake_dt)\n   621\t\n   622\t        with override_database_connection_timezone('Asia/Bangkok'):\n   623\t            event = Event.objects.get()\n   624\t            dt = datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 10, 20, 30, tzinfo=UTC)\n   625\t        self.assertEqual(event.dt, dt)\n   626\t\n   627\t    def test_write_datetime(self):\n   628\t        dt = datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 10, 20, 30, tzinfo=UTC)\n   629\t        with override_database_connection_timezone('Asia/Bangkok'):\n   630\t            Event.objects.create(dt=dt)\n   631\t\n   632\t        event = Event.objects.get()\n   633\t        fake_dt = datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 17, 20, 30, tzinfo=UTC)\n   634\t        self.assertEqual(event.dt, fake_dt)\n   635\t\n   636\t\n   637\t@override_settings(TIME_ZONE='Africa/Nairobi')\n   638\tclass SerializationTests(SimpleTestCase):\n   639\t\n   640\t    # Backend-specific notes:\n   641\t    # - JSON supports only milliseconds, microseconds will be truncated.\n   642\t    # - PyYAML dumps the UTC offset correctly for timezone-aware datetimes.\n   643\t    #   When PyYAML < 5.3 loads this representation, it subtracts the offset\n   644\t    #   and returns a naive datetime object in UTC. PyYAML 5.3+ loads timezones\n   645\t    #   correctly.\n   646\t    # Tests are adapted to take these quirks into account.\n   647\t\n   648\t    def assert_python_contains_datetime(self, objects, dt):\n   649\t        self.assertEqual(objects[0]['fields']['dt'], dt)\n   650\t\n   651\t    def assert_json_contains_datetime(self, json, dt):\n   652\t        self.assertIn('\"fields\": {\"dt\": \"%s\"}' % dt, json)\n   653\t\n   654\t    def assert_xml_contains_datetime(self, xml, dt):\n   655\t        field = parseString(xml).getElementsByTagName('field')[0]\n   656\t        self.assertXMLEqual(field.childNodes[0].wholeText, dt)\n   657\t\n   658\t    def assert_yaml_contains_datetime(self, yaml, dt):\n   659\t        # Depending on the yaml dumper, '!timestamp' might be absent\n   660\t        self.assertRegex(yaml, r\"\\n  fields: {dt: !(!timestamp)? '%s'}\" % re.escape(dt))\n   661\t\n   662\t    def test_naive_datetime(self):\n   663\t        dt = datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 13, 20, 30)\n   664\t\n   665\t        data = serializers.serialize('python', [Event(dt=dt)])\n   666\t        self.assert_python_contains_datetime(data, dt)\n   667\t        obj = next(serializers.deserialize('python', data)).object\n   668\t        self.assertEqual(obj.dt, dt)\n   669\t\n   670\t        data = serializers.serialize('json', [Event(dt=dt)])\n   671\t        self.assert_json_contains_datetime(data, \"2011-09-01T13:20:30\")\n   672\t        obj = next(serializers.deserialize('json', data)).object\n   673\t        self.assertEqual(obj.dt, dt)\n   674\t\n   675\t        data = serializers.serialize('xml', [Event(dt=dt)])\n   676\t        self.assert_xml_contains_datetime(data, \"2011-09-01T13:20:30\")\n   677\t        obj = next(serializers.deserialize('xml', data)).object\n   678\t        self.assertEqual(obj.dt, dt)\n   679\t\n   680\t        if not isinstance(serializers.get_serializer('yaml'), serializers.BadSerializer):\n   681\t            data = serializers.serialize('yaml', [Event(dt=dt)], default_flow_style=None)\n   682\t            self.assert_yaml_contains_datetime(data, \"2011-09-01 13:20:30\")\n   683\t            obj = next(serializers.deserialize('yaml', data)).object\n   684\t            self.assertEqual(obj.dt, dt)\n   685\t\n   686\t    def test_naive_datetime_with_microsecond(self):\n   687\t        dt = datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 13, 20, 30, 405060)\n   688\t\n   689\t        data = serializers.serialize('python', [Event(dt=dt)])\n   690\t        self.assert_python_contains_datetime(data, dt)\n   691\t        obj = next(serializers.deserialize('python', test_aware_datetime_with_microsecond(self):\n   711\t        dt = datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 17, 20, 30, 405060, tzinfo=ICT)\n   712\t\n   713\t        data = serializers.serialize('python',     1\t==================\n     2\tDatabase Functions\n     3\t==================\n     4\t\n     5\t.. module:: django.db.models.functions\n     6\t    :synopsis: Database Functions\n     7\t\n     8\tThe classes documented below provide a way for users to use functions provided\n     9\tby the underlying database as annotations, aggregations, or filters in Django.\n    10\tFunctions are also :doc:`expressions <expressions>`, so they can be used and\n    11\tcombined with other expressions like :ref:`aggregate functions\n    12\t<aggregation-functions>`.\n    13\t\n    14\tWe'll be using the following model in examples of each function::\n    15\t\n    16\t    class Author(models.Model):\n    17\t        name = models.CharField(max_length=50)\n    18\t        age = models.PositiveIntegerField(null=True, blank=True)\n    19\t        alias = models.CharField(max_length=50, null=True, blank=True)\n    20\t        goes_by = models.CharField(max_length=50, null=True, blank=True)\n    21\t\n    22\tWe don't usually recommend allowing ``null=True`` for ``CharField`` since this\n    23\tallows the field to have two \"empty values\", but it's important for the\n    24\t``Coalesce`` example below.\n    25\t\n    26\t.. _comparison-functions:\n    27\t\n    28\tComparison and conversion functions\n    29\t===================================\n    30\t\n    31\t``Cast``\n    32\t--------\n    33\t\n    34\t.. class:: Cast(expression, output_field)\n    35\t\n    36\tForces the result type of ``expression`` to be the one from ``output_field``.\n    37\t\n    38\tUsage example::\n    39\t\n    40\t    >>> from django.db.models import FloatField\n    41\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Cast\n    42\t    >>> Author.objects.create(age=25, name='Margaret Smith')\n    43\t    >>> author = Author.objects.annotate(\n    44\t    ...    age_as_float=Cast('age', output_field=FloatField()),\n    45\t    ... ).get()\n    46\t    >>> print(author.age_as_float)\n    47\t    25.0\n    48\t\n    49\t``Coalesce``\n    50\t------------\n    51\t\n    52\t.. class:: Coalesce(*expressions, **extra)\n    53\t\n    54\tAccepts a list of at least two field names or expressions and returns the\n    55\tfirst non-null value (note that an empty string is not considered a null\n    56\tvalue). Each argument must be of a similar type, so mixing text and numbers\n    57\twill result in a database error.\n    58\t\n    59\tUsage examples::\n    60\t\n    61\t    >>> # Get a screen name from least to most public\n    62\t    >>> from django.db.models import Sum\n    63\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Coalesce\n    64\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith', goes_by='Maggie')\n    65\t    >>> author = Author.objects.annotate(\n    66\t    ...    screen_name=Coalesce('alias', 'goes_by', 'name')).get()\n    67\t    >>> print(author.screen_name)\n    68\t    Maggie\n    69\t\n    70\t    >>> # Prevent an aggregate Sum() from returning None\n    71\t    >>> # The aggregate default argument uses Coalesce() under the hood.\n    72\t    >>> aggregated = Author.objects.aggregate(\n    73\t    ...    combined_age=Sum('age'),\n    74\t    ...    combined_age_default=Sum('age', default=0),\n    75\t    ...    combined_age_coalesce=Coalesce(Sum('age'), 0),\n    76\t    ... )\n    77\t    >>> print(aggregated['combined_age'])\n    78\t    None\n    79\t    >>> print(aggregated['combined_age_default'])\n    80\t    0\n    81\t    >>> print(aggregated['combined_age_coalesce'])\n    82\t    0\n    83\t\n    84\t.. warning::\n    85\t\n    86\t    A Python value passed to ``Coalesce`` on MySQL may be converted to an\n    87\t    incorrect type unless explicitly cast to the correct database type:\n    88\t\n    89\t    >>> from django.db.models import DateTimeField\n    90\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Cast, Coalesce\n    91\t    >>> from django.utils import timezone\n    92\t    >>> now = timezone.now()\n    93\t    >>> Coalesce('updated', Cast(now, DateTimeField()))\n    94\t\n    95\t``Collate``\n    96\t-----------\n    97\t\n    98\t.. class:: Collate(expression, collation)\n    99\t\n   100\t.. versionadded:: 3.2\n   101\t\n   102\tTakes an expression and a collation name to query against.\n   103\t\n   104\tFor example, to filter case-insensitively in SQLite::\n   105\t\n   106\t    >>> Author.objects.filter(name=Collate(Value('john'), 'nocase'))\n   107\t    <QuerySet [<Author: John>, <Author: john>]>\n   108\t\n   109\tIt can also be used when ordering, for example with PostgreSQL::\n   110\t\n   111\t    >>> Author.objects.order_by(Collate('name', 'et-x-icu'))\n   112\t    <QuerySet [<Author: Ursula>, <Author: Veronika>, <Author: Ülle>]>\n   113\t\n   114\t``Greatest``\n   115\t------------\n   116\t\n   117\t.. class:: Greatest(*expressions, **extra)\n   118\t\n   119\tAccepts a list of at least two field names or expressions and returns the\n   120\tgreatest value. Each argument must be of a similar type, so mixing text and\n   121\tnumbers will result in a database error.\n   122\t\n   123\tUsage example::\n   124\t\n   125\t    class Blog(models.Model):\n   126\t        body = models.TextField()\n   127\t        modified = models.DateTimeField(auto_now=True)\n   128\t\n   129\t    class Comment(models.Model):\n   130\t        body = models.TextField()\n   131\t        modified = models.DateTimeField(auto_now=True)\n   132\t        blog = models.ForeignKey(Blog, on_delete=models.CASCADE)\n   133\t\n   134\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Greatest\n   135\t    >>> blog = Blog.objects.create(body='Greatest is the best.')\n   136\t    >>> comment = Comment.objects.create(body='No, Least is better.', blog=blog)\n   137\t    >>> comments = Comment.objects.annotate(last_updated=Greatest('modified', 'blog__modified'))\n   138\t    >>> annotated_comment = comments.get()\n   139\t\n   140\t``annotated_comment.last_updated`` will be the most recent of ``blog.modified``\n   141\tand ``comment.modified``.\n   142\t\n   143\t.. warning::\n   144\t\n   145\t    The behavior of ``Greatest`` when one or more expression may be ``null``\n   146\t    varies between databases:\n   147\t\n   148\t    - PostgreSQL: ``Greatest`` will return the largest non-null expression,\n   149\t      or ``null`` if all expressions are ``null``.\n   150\t    - SQLite, Oracle, and MySQL: If any expression is ``null``, ``Greatest``\n   151\t      will return ``null``.\n   152\t\n   153\t    The PostgreSQL behavior can be emulated using ``Coalesce`` if you know\n   154\t    a sensible minimum value to provide as a default.\n   155\t\n   156\t``JSONObject``\n   157\t--------------\n   158\t\n   159\t.. class:: JSONObject(**fields)\n   160\t\n   161\t.. versionadded:: 3.2\n   162\t\n   163\tTakes a list of key-value pairs and returns a JSON object containing those\n   164\tpairs.\n   165\t\n   166\tUsage example::\n   167\t\n   168\t    >>> from django.db.models import F\n   169\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import JSONObject, Lower\n   170\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith', alias='msmith', age=25)\n   171\t    >>> author = Author.objects.annotate(json_object=JSONObject(\n   172\t    ...     name=Lower('name'),\n   173\t    ...     alias='alias',\n   174\t    ...     age=F('age') * 2,\n   175\t    ... )).get()\n   176\t    >>> author.json_object\n   177\t    {'name': 'margaret smith', 'alias': 'msmith', 'age': 50}\n   178\t\n   179\t``Least``\n   180\t---------\n   181\t\n   182\t.. class:: Least(*expressions, **extra)\n   183\t\n   184\tAccepts a list of at least two field names or expressions and returns the\n   185\tleast value. Each argument must be of a similar type, so mixing text and numbers\n   186\twill result in a database error.\n   187\t\n   188\t.. warning::\n   189\t\n   190\t    The behavior of ``Least`` when one or more expression may be ``null``\n   191\t    varies between databases:\n   192\t\n   193\t    - PostgreSQL: ``Least`` will return the smallest non-null expression,\n   194\t      or ``null`` if all expressions are ``null``.\n   195\t    - SQLite, Oracle, and MySQL: If any expression is ``null``, ``Least``\n   196\t      will return ``null``.\n   197\t\n   198\t    The PostgreSQL behavior can be emulated using ``Coalesce`` if you know\n   199\t    a sensible maximum value to provide as a default.\n   200\t\n   201\t``NullIf``\n   202\t----------\n   203\t\n   204\t.. class:: NullIf(expression1, expression2)\n   205\t\n   206\tAccepts two expressions and returns ``None`` if they are equal, otherwise\n   207\treturns ``expression1``.\n   208\t\n   209\t.. admonition:: Caveats on Oracle\n   210\t\n   211\t    Due to an :ref:`Oracle convention<oracle-null-empty-strings>`, this\n   212\t    function returns the empty string instead of ``None`` when the expressions\n   213\t    are of type :class:`~django.db.models.CharField`.\n   214\t\n   215\t    Passing ``Value(None)`` to ``expression1`` is prohibited on Oracle since\n   216\t    Oracle doesn't accept ``NULL`` as the first argument.\n   217\t\n   218\t.. _date-functions:\n   219\t\n   220\tDate functions\n   221\t==============\n   222\t\n   223\tWe'll be using the following model in examples of each function::\n   224\t\n   225\t    class Experiment(models.Model):\n   226\t        start_datetime = models.DateTimeField()\n   227\t        start_date = models.DateField(null=True, blank=True)\n   228\t        start_time = models.TimeField(null=True, blank=True)\n   229\t        end_datetime = models.DateTimeField(null=True, blank=True)\n   230\t        end_date = models.DateField(null=True, blank=True)\n   231\t        end_time = models.TimeField(null=True, blank=True)\n   232\t\n   233\t``Extract``\n   234\t-----------\n   235\t\n   236\t.. class:: Extract(expression, lookup_name=None, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   237\t\n   238\tExtracts a component of a date as a number.\n   239\t\n   240\tTakes an ``expression`` representing a ``DateField``, ``DateTimeField``,\n   241\t``TimeField``, or ``DurationField`` and a ``lookup_name``, and returns the part\n   242\tof the date referenced by ``lookup_name`` as an ``IntegerField``.\n   243\tDjango usually uses the databases' extract function, so you may use any\n   244\t``lookup_name`` that your database supports. A ``tzinfo`` subclass, usually\n   245\tprovided by ``pytz``, can be passed to extract a value in a specific timezone.\n   246\t\n   247\tGiven the datetime ``2015-06-15 23:30:01.000321+00:00``, the built-in\n   248\t``lookup_name``\\s return:\n   249\t\n   250\t* \"year\": 2015\n   251\t* \"iso_year\": 2015\n   252\t* \"quarter\": 2\n   253\t* \"month\": 6\n   254\t* \"day\": 15\n   255\t* \"week\": 25\n   256\t* \"week_day\": 2\n   257\t* \"iso_week_day\": 1\n   258\t* \"hour\": 23\n   259\t* \"minute\": 30\n   260\t* \"second\": 1\n   261\t\n   262\tIf a different timezone like ``Australia/Melbourne`` is active in Django, then\n   263\tthe datetime is converted to the timezone before the value is extracted. The\n   264\ttimezone offset for Melbourne in the example date above is +10:00. The values\n   265\treturned when this timezone is active will be the same as above except for:\n   266\t\n   267\t* \"day\": 16\n   268\t* \"week_day\": 3\n   269\t* \"iso_week_day\": 2\n   270\t* \"hour\": 9\n   271\t\n   272\t.. admonition:: ``week_day`` values\n   273\t\n   274\t    The ``week_day`` ``lookup_type`` is calculated differently from most\n   275\t    databases and from Python's standard functions. This function will return\n   276\t    ``1`` for Sunday, ``2`` for Monday, through ``7`` for Saturday.\n   277\t\n   278\t    The equivalent calculation in Python is::\n   279\t\n   280\t        >>> from datetime import datetime\n   281\t        >>> dt = datetime(2015, 6, 15)\n   282\t        >>> (dt.isoweekday() % 7) + 1\n   283\t        2\n   284\t\n   285\t.. admonition:: ``week`` values\n   286\t\n   287\t    The ``week`` ``lookup_type`` is calculated based on `ISO-8601\n   288\t    <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO-8601>`_, i.e.,\n   289\t    a week starts on a Monday. The first week of a year is the one that\n   290\t    contains the year's first Thursday, i.e. the first week has the majority\n   291\t    (four or more) of its days in the year. The value returned is in the range\n   292\t    1 to 52 or 53.\n   293\t\n   294\tEach ``lookup_name`` above has a corresponding ``Extract`` subclass (listed\n   295\tbelow) that should typically be used instead of the more verbose equivalent,\n   296\te.g. use ``ExtractYear(...)`` rather than ``Extract(..., lookup_name='year')``.\n   297\t\n   298\tUsage example::\n   299\t\n   300\t    >>> from datetime import datetime\n   301\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Extract\n   302\t    >>> start = datetime(2015, 6, 15)\n   303\t    >>> end = datetime(2015, 7, 2)\n   304\t    >>> Experiment.objects.create(\n   305\t    ...    start_datetime=start, start_date=start.date(),\n   306\t    ...    end_datetime=end, end_date=end.date())\n   307\t    >>> # Add the experiment start year as a field in the QuerySet.\n   308\t    >>> experiment = Experiment.objects.annotate(\n   309\t    ...    start_year=Extract('start_datetime', 'year')).get()\n   310\t    >>> experiment.start_year\n   311\t    2015\n   312\t    >>> # How many experiments completed in the same year in which they started?\n   313\t    >>> Experiment.objects.filter(\n   314\t    ...    start_datetime__year=Extract('end_datetime', 'year')).count()\n   315\t    1\n   316\t\n   317\t``DateField`` extracts\n   318\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   319\t\n   320\t.. class:: ExtractYear(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   321\t\n   322\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'year'\n   323\t\n   324\t.. class:: ExtractIsoYear(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   325\t\n   326\t    Returns the ISO-8601 week-numbering year.\n   327\t\n   328\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'iso_year'\n   329\t\n   330\t.. class:: ExtractMonth(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   331\t\n   332\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'month'\n   333\t\n   334\t.. class:: ExtractDay(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   335\t\n   336\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'day'\n   337\t\n   338\t.. class:: ExtractWeekDay(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   339\t\n   340\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'week_day'\n   341\t\n   342\t.. class:: ExtractIsoWeekDay(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   343\t\n   344\t    Returns the ISO-8601 week day with day 1 being Monday and day 7 being\n   345\t    Sunday.\n   346\t\n   347\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'iso_week_day'\n   348\t\n   349\t.. class:: ExtractWeek(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   350\t\n   351\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'week'\n   352\t\n   353\t.. class:: ExtractQuarter(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   354\t\n   355\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'quarter'\n   356\t\n   357\tThese are logically equivalent to ``Extract('date_field', lookup_name)``. Each\n   358\tclass is also a ``Transform`` registered on ``DateField`` and ``DateTimeField``\n   359\tas ``__(lookup_name)``, e.g. ``__year``.\n   360\t\n   361\tSince ``DateField``\\s don't have a time component, only ``Extract`` subclasses\n   362\tthat deal with date-parts can be used with ``DateField``::\n   363\t\n   364\t    >>> from datetime import datetime\n   365\t    >>> from django.utils import timezone\n   366\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import (\n   367\t    ...     ExtractDay, ExtractMonth, ExtractQuarter, ExtractWeek,\n   368\t    ...     ExtractIsoWeekDay, ExtractWeekDay, ExtractIsoYear, ExtractYear,\n   369\t    ... )\n   370\t    >>> start_2015 = datetime(2015, 6, 15, 23, 30, 1, tzinfo=timezone.utc)\n   371\t    >>> end_2015 = datetime(2015, 6, 16, 13, 11, 27, tzinfo=timezone.utc)\n   372\t    >>> Experiment.objects.create(\n   373\t    ...    start_datetime=start_2015, start_date=start_2015.date(),\n   374\t    ...    end_datetime=end_2015, end_date=end_2015.date())\n   375\t    >>> Experiment.objects.annotate(\n   376\t    ...     year=ExtractYear('start_date'),\n   377\t    ...     isoyear=ExtractIsoYear('start_date'),\n   378\t    ...     quarter=ExtractQuarter('start_date'),\n   379\t    ...     month=ExtractMonth('start_date'),\n   380\t    ...     week=ExtractWeek('start_date'),\n   381\t    ...     day=ExtractDay('start_date'),\n   382\t    ...     weekday=ExtractWeekDay('start_date'),\n   383\t    ...     isoweekday=ExtractIsoWeekDay('start_date'),\n   384\t    ... ).values(\n   385\t    ...     'year', 'isoyear', 'quarter', 'month', 'week', 'day', 'weekday',\n   386\t    ...     'isoweekday',\n   387\t    ... ).get(end_date__year=ExtractYear('start_date'))\n   388\t    {'year': 2015, 'isoyear': 2015, 'quarter': 2, 'month': 6, 'week': 25,\n   389\t     'day': 15, 'weekday': 2, 'isoweekday': 1}\n   390\t\n   391\t``DateTimeField`` extracts\n   392\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   393\t\n   394\tIn addition to the following, all extracts for ``DateField`` listed above may\n   395\talso be used on ``DateTimeField``\\s .\n   396\t\n   397\t.. class:: ExtractHour(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   398\t\n   399\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'hour'\n   400\t\n   401\t.. class:: ExtractMinute(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   402\t\n   403\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'minute'\n   404\t\n   405\t.. class:: ExtractSecond(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   406\t\n   407\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'second'\n   408\t\n   409\tThese are logically equivalent to ``Extract('datetime_field', lookup_name)``.\n   410\tEach class is also a ``Transform`` registered on ``DateTimeField`` as\n   411\t``__(lookup_name)``, e.g. ``__minute``.\n   412\t\n   413\t``DateTimeField`` examples::\n   414\t\n   415\t    >>> from datetime import datetime\n   416\t    >>> from django.utils import timezone\n   417\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import (\n   418\t    ...     ExtractDay, ExtractHour, ExtractMinute, ExtractMonth,\n   419\t    ...     ExtractQuarter, ExtractSecond, ExtractWeek, ExtractIsoWeekDay,\n   420\t    ...     ExtractWeekDay, ExtractIsoYear, ExtractYear,\n   421\t    ... )\n   422\t    >>> start_2015 = datetime(2015, 6, 15, 23, 30, 1, tzinfo=timezone.utc)\n   423\t    >>> end_2015 = datetime(2015, 6, 16, 13, 11, 27, tzinfo=timezone.utc)\n   424\t    >>> Experiment.objects.create(\n   425\t    ...    start_datetime=start_2015, start_date=start_2015.date(),\n   426\t    ...    end_datetime=end_2015, end_date=end_2015.date())\n   427\t    >>> Experiment.objects.annotate(\n   428\t    ...     year=ExtractYear('start_datetime'),\n   429\t    ...     isoyear=ExtractIsoYear('start_datetime'),\n   430\t    ...     quarter=ExtractQuarter('start_datetime'),\n   431\t    ...     month=ExtractMonth('start_datetime'),\n   432\t    ...     week=ExtractWeek('start_datetime'),\n   433\t    ...     day=ExtractDay('start_datetime'),\n   434\t    ...     weekday=ExtractWeekDay('start_datetime'),\n   435\t    ...     isoweekday=ExtractIsoWeekDay('start_datetime'),\n   436\t    ...     hour=ExtractHour('start_datetime'),\n   437\t    ...     minute=ExtractMinute('start_datetime'),\n   438\t    ...     second=ExtractSecond('start_datetime'),\n   439\t    ... ).values(\n   440\t    ...     'year', 'isoyear', 'month', 'week', 'day',\n   441\t    ...     'weekday', 'isoweekday', 'hour', 'minute', 'second',\n   442\t    ... ).get(end_datetime__year=ExtractYear('start_datetime'))\n   443\t    {'year': 2015, 'isoyear': 2015, 'quarter': 2, 'month': 6, 'week': 25,\n   444\t     'day': 15, 'weekday': 2, 'isoweekday': 1, 'hour': 23, 'minute': 30,\n   445\t     'second': 1}\n   446\t\n   447\tWhen :setting:`USE_TZ` is ``True`` then datetimes are stored in the database\n   448\tin UTC. If a different timezone is active in Django, the datetime is converted\n   449\tto that timezone before the value is extracted. The example below converts to\n   450\tthe Melbourne timezone (UTC +10:00), which changes the day, weekday, and hour\n   451\tvalues that are returned::\n   452\t\n   453\t    >>> import pytz\n   454\t    >>> melb = pytz.timezone('Australia/Melbourne')  # UTC+10:00\n   455\t    >>> with timezone.override(melb):\n   456\t    ...    Experiment.objects.annotate(\n   457\t    ...        day=ExtractDay('start_datetime'),\n   458\t    ...        weekday=ExtractWeekDay('start_datetime'),\n   459\t    ...        isoweekday=ExtractIsoWeekDay('start_datetime'),\n   460\t    ...        hour=ExtractHour('start_datetime'),\n   461\t    ...    ).values('day', 'weekday', 'isoweekday', 'hour').get(\n   462\t    ...        end_datetime__year=ExtractYear('start_datetime'),\n   463\t    ...    )\n   464\t    {'day': 16, 'weekday': 3, 'isoweekday': 2, 'hour': 9}\n   465\t\n   466\tExplicitly passing the timezone to the ``Extract`` function behaves in the same\n   467\tway, and takes priority over an active timezone::\n   468\t\n   469\t    >>> import pytz\n   470\t    >>> melb = pytz.timezone('Australia/Melbourne')\n   471\t    >>> Experiment.objects.annotate(\n   472\t    ...     day=ExtractDay('start_datetime', tzinfo=melb),\n   473\t    ...     weekday=ExtractWeekDay('start_datetime', tzinfo=melb),\n   474\t    ...     isoweekday=ExtractIsoWeekDay('start_datetime', tzinfo=melb),\n   475\t    ...     hour=ExtractHour('start_datetime', tzinfo=melb),\n   476\t    ... ).values('day', 'weekday', 'isoweekday', 'hour').get(\n   477\t    ...     end_datetime__year=ExtractYear('start_datetime'),\n   478\t    ... )\n   479\t    {'day': 16, 'weekday': 3, 'isoweekday': 2, 'hour': 9}\n   480\t\n   481\t``Now``\n   482\t-------\n   483\t\n   484\t.. class:: Now()\n   485\t\n   486\tReturns the database server's current date and time when the query is executed,\n   487\ttypically using the SQL ``CURRENT_TIMESTAMP``.\n   488\t\n   489\tUsage example::\n   490\t\n   491\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Now\n   492\t    >>> Article.objects.filter(published__lte=Now())\n   493\t    <QuerySet [<Article: How to Django>]>\n   494\t\n   495\t.. admonition:: PostgreSQL considerations\n   496\t\n   497\t    On PostgreSQL, the SQL ``CURRENT_TIMESTAMP`` returns the time that the\n   498\t    current transaction started. Therefore for cross-database compatibility,\n   499\t    ``Now()`` uses ``STATEMENT_TIMESTAMP`` instead. If you need the transaction\n   500\t    timestamp, use :class:`django.contrib.postgres.functions.TransactionNow`.\n   501\t\n   502\t``Trunc``\n   503\t---------\n   504\t\n   505\t.. class:: Trunc(expression, kind, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   506\t\n   507\tTruncates a date up to a significant component.\n   508\t\n   509\tWhen you only care if something happened in a particular year, hour, or day,\n   510\tbut not the exact second, then ``Trunc`` (and its subclasses) can be useful to\n   511\tfilter or aggregate your data. For example, you can use ``Trunc`` to calculate\n   512\tthe number of sales per day.\n   513\t\n   514\t``Trunc`` takes a single ``expression``, representing a ``DateField``,\n   515\t``TimeField``, or ``DateTimeField``, a ``kind`` representing a date or time\n   516\tpart, and an ``output_field`` that's either ``DateTimeField()``,\n   517\t``TimeField()``, or ``DateField()``. It returns a datetime, date, or time\n   518\tdepending on ``output_field``, with fields up to ``kind`` set to their minimum\n   519\tvalue. If ``output_field`` is omitted, it will default to the ``output_field``\n   520\tof ``expression``. A ``tzinfo`` subclass, usually provided by ``pytz``, can be\n   521\tpassed to truncate a value in a specific timezone.\n   522\t\n   523\tThe ``is_dst`` parameter indicates whether or not ``pytz`` should interpret\n   524\tnonexistent and ambiguous datetimes in daylight saving time. By default (when\n   525\t``is_dst=None``), ``pytz`` raises an exception for such datetimes.\n   526\t\n   527\tGiven the datetime ``2015-06-15 14:30:50.000321+00:00``, the built-in ``kind``\\s\n   528\treturn:\n   529\t\n   530\t* \"year\": 2015-01-01 00:00:00+00:00\n   531\t* \"quarter\": 2015-04-01 00:00:00+00:00\n   532\t* \"month\": 2015-06-01 00:00:00+00:00\n   533\t* \"week\": 2015-06-15 00:00:00+00:00\n   534\t* \"day\": 2015-06-15 00:00:00+00:00\n   535\t* \"hour\": 2015-06-15 14:00:00+00:00\n   536\t* \"minute\": 2015-06-15 14:30:00+00:00\n   537\t* \"second\": 2015-06-15 14:30:50+00:00\n   538\t\n   539\tIf a different timezone like ``Australia/Melbourne`` is active in Django, then\n   540\tthe datetime is converted to the new timezone before the value is truncated.\n   541\tThe timezone offset for Melbourne in the example date above is +10:00. The\n   542\tvalues returned when this timezone is active will be:\n   543\t\n   544\t* \"year\": 2015-01-01 00:00:00+11:00\n   545\t* \"quarter\": 2015-04-01 00:00:00+10:00\n   546\t* \"month\": 2015-06-01 00:00:00+10:00\n   547\t* \"week\": 2015-06-16 00:00:00+10:00\n   548\t* \"day\": 2015-06-16 00:00:00+10:00\n   549\t* \"hour\": 2015-06-16 00:00:00+10:00\n   550\t* \"minute\": 2015-06-16 00:30:00+10:00\n   551\t* \"second\": 2015-06-16 00:30:50+10:00\n   552\t\n   553\tThe year has an offset of +11:00 because the result transitioned into daylight\n   554\tsaving time.\n   555\t\n   556\tEach ``kind`` above has a corresponding ``Trunc`` subclass (listed below) that\n   557\tshould typically be used instead of the more verbose equivalent,\n   558\te.g. use ``TruncYear(...)`` rather than ``Trunc(..., kind='year')``.\n   559\t\n   560\tThe subclasses are all defined as transforms, but they aren't registered with\n   561\tany fields, because the lookup names are already reserved by the ``Extract``\n   562\tsubclasses.\n   563\t\n   564\tUsage example::\n   565\t\n   566\t    >>> from datetime import datetime\n   567\t    >>> from django.db.models import Count, DateTimeField\n   568\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Trunc\n   569\t    >>> Experiment.objects.create(start_datetime=datetime(2015, 6, 15, 14, 30, 50, 321))\n   570\t    >>> Experiment.objects.create(start_datetime=datetime(2015, 6, 15, 14, 40, 2, 123))\n   571\t    >>> Experiment.objects.create(start_datetime=datetime(2015, 12, 25, 10, 5, 27, 999))\n   572\t    >>> experiments_per_day = Experiment.objects.annotate(\n   573\t    ...    start_day=Trunc('start_datetime', 'day', output_field=DateTimeField())\n   574\t    ... ).values('start_day').annotate(experiments=Count('id'))\n   575\t    >>> for exp in experiments_per_day:\n   576\t    ...     print(exp['start_day'], exp['experiments'])\n   577\t    ...\n   578\t    2015-06-15 00:00:00 2\n   579\t    2015-12-25 00:00:00 1\n   580\t    >>> experiments = Experiment.objects.annotate(\n   581\t    ...    start_day=Trunc('start_datetime', 'day', output_field=DateTimeField())\n   582\t    ... ).filter(start_day=datetime(2015, 6, 15))\n   583\t    >>> for exp in experiments:\n   584\t    ...     print(exp.start_datetime)\n   585\t    ...\n   586\t    2015-06-15 14:30:50.000321\n   587\t    2015-06-15 14:40:02.000123\n   588\t\n   589\t``DateField`` truncation\n   590\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   591\t\n   592\t.. class:: TruncYear(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   593\t\n   594\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'year'\n   595\t\n   596\t.. class:: TruncMonth(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   597\t\n   598\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'month'\n   599\t\n   600\t.. class:: TruncWeek(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   601\t\n   602\t    Truncates to midnight on the Monday of the week.\n   603\t\n   604\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'week'\n   605\t\n   606\t.. class:: TruncQuarter(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   607\t\n   608\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'quarter'\n   609\t\n   610\tThese are logically equivalent to ``Trunc('date_field', kind)``. They truncate\n   611\tall parts of the date up to ``kind`` which allows grouping or filtering dates\n   612\twith less precision. ``expression`` can have an ``output_field`` of either\n   613\t``DateField`` or ``DateTimeField``.\n   614\t\n   615\tSince ``DateField``\\s don't have a time component, only ``Trunc`` subclasses\n   616\tthat deal with date-parts can be used with ``DateField``::\n   617\t\n   618\t    >>> from datetime import datetime\n   619\t    >>> from django.db.models import Count\n   620\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import TruncMonth, TruncYear\n   621\t    >>> from django.utils import timezone\n   622\t    >>> start1 = datetime(2014, 6, 15, 14, 30, 50, 321, tzinfo=timezone.utc)\n   623\t    >>> start2 = datetime(2015, 6, 15, 14, 40, 2, 123, tzinfo=timezone.utc)\n   624\t    >>> start3 = datetime(2015, 12, 31, 17, 5, 27, 999, tzinfo=timezone.utc)\n   625\t    >>> Experiment.objects.create(start_datetime=start1, start_date=start1.date())\n   626\t    >>> Experiment.objects.create(start_datetime=start2, start_date=start2.date())\n   627\t    >>> Experiment.objects.create(start_datetime=start3, start_date=start3.date())\n   628\t    >>> experiments_per_year = Experiment.objects.annotate(\n   629\t    ...    year=TruncYear('start_date')).values('year').annotate(\n   630\t    ...    experiments=Count('id'))\n   631\t    >>> for exp in experiments_per_year:\n   632\t    ...     print(exp['year'], exp['experiments'])\n   633\t    ...\n   634\t    2014-01-01 1\n   635\t    2015-01-01 2\n   636\t\n   637\t    >>> import pytz\n   638\t    >>> melb = pytz.timezone('Australia/Melbourne')\n   639\t    >>> experiments_per_month = Experiment.objects.annotate(\n   640\t    ...    month=TruncMonth('start_datetime', tzinfo=melb)).values('month').annotate(\n   641\t    ...    experiments=Count('id'))\n   642\t    >>> for exp in experiments_per_month:\n   643\t    ...     print(exp['month'], exp['experiments'])\n   644\t    ...\n   645\t    2015-06-01 00:00:00+10:00 1\n   646\t    2016-01-01 00:00:00+11:00 1\n   647\t    2014-06-01 00:00:00+10:00 1\n   648\t\n   649\t``DateTimeField`` truncation\n   650\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   651\t\n   652\t.. class:: TruncDate(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   653\t\n   654\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'date'\n   655\t    .. attribute:: output_field = DateField()\n   656\t\n   657\t    .. versionchanged:: 3.2\n   658\t\n   659\t        The ``tzinfo`` parameter was added.\n   660\t\n   661\t``TruncDate`` casts ``expression`` to a date rather than using the built-in SQL\n   662\ttruncate function. It's also registered as a transform on  ``DateTimeField`` as\n   663\t``__date``.\n   664\t\n   665\t.. class:: TruncTime(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   666\t\n   667\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'time'\n   668\t    .. attribute:: output_field = TimeField()\n   669\t\n   670\t    .. versionchanged:: 3.2\n   671\t\n   672\t        The ``tzinfo`` parameter was added.\n   673\t\n   674\t``TruncTime`` casts ``expression`` to a time rather than using the built-in SQL\n   675\ttruncate function. It's also registered as a transform on ``DateTimeField`` as\n   676\t``__time``.\n   677\t\n   678\t.. class:: TruncDay(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   679\t\n   680\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'day'\n   681\t\n   682\t.. class:: TruncHour(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   683\t\n   684\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'hour'\n   685\t\n   686\t.. class:: TruncMinute(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   687\t\n   688\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'minute'\n   689\t\n   690\t.. class:: TruncSecond(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   691\t\n   692\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'second'\n   693\t\n   694\tThese are logically equivalent to ``Trunc('datetime_field', kind)``. They\n   695\ttruncate all parts of the date up to ``kind`` and allow grouping or filtering\n   696\tdatetimes with less precision. ``expression`` must have an ``output_field`` of\n   697\t``DateTimeField``.\n   698\t\n   699\tUsage example::\n   700\t\n   701\t    >>> from datetime import date, datetime\n   702\t    >>> from django.db.models import Count\n   703\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import (\n   704\t    ...     TruncDate, TruncDay, TruncHour, TruncMinute, TruncSecond,\n   705\t    ... )\n   706\t    >>> from django.utils import timezone\n   707\t    >>> import pytz\n   708\t    >>> start1 = datetime(2014, 6, 15, 14, 30, 50, 321, tzinfo=timezone.utc)\n   709\t    >>> Experiment.objects.create(start_datetime=start1, start_date=start1.date())\n   710\t    >>> melb = pytz.timezone('Australia/Melbourne')\n   711\t    >>> Experiment.objects.annotate(\n   712\t    ...     date=TruncDate('start_datetime'),\n   713\t    ...     day=TruncDay('start_datetime', tzinfo=melb),\n   714\t    ...     hour=TruncHour('start_datetime', tzinfo=melb),\n   715\t    ...     minute=TruncMinute('start_datetime'),\n   716\t    ...     second=TruncSecond('start_datetime'),\n   717\t    ... ).values('date', 'day', 'hour', 'minute', 'second').get()\n   718\t    {'date': datetime.date(2014, 6, 15),\n   719\t     'day': datetime.datetime(2014, 6, 16, 0, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Australia/Melbourne' AEST+10:00:00 STD>),\n   720\t     'hour': datetime.datetime(2014, 6, 16, 0, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Australia/Melbourne' AEST+10:00:00 STD>),\n   721\t     'minute': 'minute': datetime.datetime(2014, 6, 15, 14, 30, tzinfo=<UTC>),\n   722\t     'second': datetime.datetime(2014, 6, 15, 14, 30, 50, tzinfo=<UTC>)\n   723\t    }\n   724\t\n   725\t``TimeField`` truncation\n   726\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   727\t\n   728\t.. class:: TruncHour(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   729\t    :noindex:\n   730\t\n   731\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'hour'\n   732\t\n   733\t.. class:: TruncMinute(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   734\t    :noindex:\n   735\t\n   736\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'minute'\n   737\t\n   738\t.. class:: TruncSecond(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   739\t    :noindex:\n   740\t\n   741\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'second'\n   742\t\n   743\tThese are logically equivalent to ``Trunc('time_field', kind)``. They truncate\n   744\tall parts of the time up to ``kind`` which allows grouping or filtering times\n   745\twith less precision. ``expression`` can have an ``output_field`` of either\n   746\t``TimeField`` or ``DateTimeField``.\n   747\t\n   748\tSince ``TimeField``\\s don't have a date component, only ``Trunc`` subclasses\n   749\tthat deal with time-parts can be used with 783\tMath Functions\n   784\t==============\n   785\t\n   786\tWe'll be using the following model in math function examples::\n   787\t\n   788\t    class Vector(models.Model):\n   789\t        x = models.FloatField()\n   790\t        y = models.FloatField()\n   791\t\n   792\t``Abs``\n   793\t-------\n   794\t\n   795\t.. class:: Abs(expression, **extra)\n   796\t\n   797\tReturns the absolute value of a numeric field or expression.\n   798\t\n   799\tUsage example::\n   800\t\n   801\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Abs\n   802\t    >>> Vector.objects.create(x=-0.5, y=1.1)\n as a transform. For example::\n   808\t\n   809\t    >>> from django.db.models import FloatField\n   810\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Abs\n   811\t    >>> FloatField.register_lookup(Abs)\n   812\t    >>> # Get vectors inside the unit cube\n   813\t    >>> vectors = Vector.objects.filter(x__abs__lt=1, y__abs__lt=1)\n   814\t\n   815\t``ACos``\n   816\t--------\n   817\t\n   818\t.. class:: ACos(expression, **extra)\n   819\t\n   820\tReturns the arccosine of a numeric field or expression. Note that these\n  2710\tformat strings use Python's :ref:`datetime module syntax\n  2711\t<strftime-strptime-behavior>`, not the format strings from the :tfilter:`date`\n  2712\ttemplate filter.\n  2713\t\n  2714\tWhen :setting:`USE_L10N` is ``True``, the locale-dictated format has higher\n  2715\tprecedence and will be applied instead.\n  2716\t\n  2717\tSee also :setting:`DATE_INPUT_FORMATS` and :setting:`DATETIME_INPUT_FORMATS`.\n  2718\t\n  2719\t.. setting:: TIME_ZONE\n  2720\t\n  2721\t``TIME_ZONE``\n  2722\t-------------\n  2723\t\n  2724\tDefault: ``'America/Chicago'``\n  2725\t\n  2726\tA string representing the time zone for this installation. See the `list of\n  2727\ttime zones`_.\n  2728\t\n  2729\t.. note::\n  2730\t    Since Django was first released with the :setting:`TIME_ZONE` set to\n  2731\t    ``'America/Chicago'``, the global setting (used if nothing is defined in\n  2732\t    your project's ``settings.py``) remains ``'America/Chicago'`` for backwards\n  2733\t    compatibility. New project templates default to ``'UTC'``.\n  2734\t\n  2735\tNote that this isn't necessarily the time zone of the server. For example, one\n  2736\tserver may serve multiple Django-powered sites, each with a separate time zone\n  2737\tsetting.\n  2738\t\n  2739\tWhen :setting:`USE_TZ` is ``False``, this is the time zone in which Django\n  2740\twill store all datetimes. When :setting:`USE_TZ` is ``True``, this is the\n  2741\tdefault time zone that Django will use to display datetimes in templates and\n  2742\tto interpret datetimes entered in forms.\n  2743\t\n  2744\tOn Unix environments (where :func:`time.tzset` is implemented), Django sets the\n  2745\t``os.environ['TZ']`` variable to the time zone you specify in the\n  2746\t:setting:`TIME_ZONE` setting. Thus, all your views and models will\n  2747\tautomatically operate in this time zone. However, Django won't set the ``TZ``\n  2748\tenvironment variable if you're using the manual configuration option as\n  2749\tdescribed in :ref:`manually configuring settings\n  2750\t<settings-without-django-settings-module>`. If Django doesn't set the ``TZ``\n  2751\tenvironment variable, it's up to you to ensure your processes are running in\n  2752\tthe correct environment.\n  2753\t\n  2754\t.. note::\n  2755\t    Django cannot reliably use alternate time zones in a Windows environment.\n  2756\t    If you're running Django on Windows, :setting:`TIME_ZONE` must be set to\n  2757\t    match the system time zone.\n  2758\t\n  2759\t.. _list of time zones: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_tz_database_time_zones\n  2760\t\n  2761\t.. setting:: USE_I18N\n  2762\t\n  2763\t``USE_I18N``\n  2764\t------------\n  2765\t\n  2766\tDefault: ``True``\n  2767\t\n  2768\tA boolean that specifies whether Django's translation system should be enabled.\n  2769\tThis provides a way to turn it off, for performance. If this is set to\n  2770\t``False``, Django will make some optimizations so as not to load the\n  2771\ttranslation machinery.\n  2772\t\n  2773\tSee also :setting:`LANGUAGE_CODE`, :setting:`USE_L10N` and :setting:`USE_TZ`.\n  2774\t\n  2775\t.. note::\n  2776\t\n  2777\t    The default :file:`settings.py` file created by :djadmin:`django-admin\n  2778\t    startproject <startproject>` includes ``USE_I18N = True`` for convenience.\n  2779\t\n  2780\t.. setting:: USE_L10N\n  2781\t\n  2782\t``USE_L10N``\n  2783\t------------\n  2784\t\n  2785\tDefault: ``True``\n  2786\t\n  2787\tA boolean that specifies if localized formatting of data will be enabled by\n  2788\tdefault or not. If this is set to ``True``, e.g. Django will display numbers and\n  2789\tdates using the format of the current locale.\n  2790\t\n  2791\tSee also :setting:`LANGUAGE_CODE`, :setting:`USE_I18N` and :setting:`USE_TZ`.\n  2792\t\n  2793\t.. versionchanged:: 4.0\n  2794\t\n  2795\t    In older versions, the default value is ``False``.\n  2796\t\n  2797\t.. deprecated:: 4.0\n  2798\t\n  2799\t    This setting is deprecated. Starting with Django 5.0, localized formatting\n  2800\t    of data will always be enabled. For example Django will display numbers and\n  2801\t    dates using the format of the current locale.\n  2802\t\n  2803\t.. setting:: USE_THOUSAND_SEPARATOR\n  2804\t\n  2805\t``USE_THOUSAND_SEPARATOR``\n  2806\t--------------------------\n  2807\t\n  2808\tDefault: ``False``\n  2809\t\n  2810\tA boolean that specifies whether to display numbers using a thousand separator.\n  2811\tWhen set to ``True`` and :setting:`USE_L10N` is also ``True``, Django will\n  2812\tformat numbers using the :setting:`NUMBER_GROUPING` and\n  2813\t:setting:`THOUSAND_SEPARATOR` settings. isinstance(offset, timedelta):\\n        offset = offset.total_seconds() // 60\\n    sign = '-' if offset < 0 else '+'\\n    hhmm = '%02d%02d' % divmod(abs(offset), 60)\\n    name = sign + hhmm\\n get_default_timezone():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Return the default time zone as a tzinfo instance.\\n\\n    This is the time zone defined by settings.TIME_ZONE.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    if pytz is not None and getattr(settings, 'USE_DEPRECATED_PYTZ', False):\\n        return pytz.timezone(settings.TIME_ZONE)\\n    return zoneinfo.ZoneInfo(settings.TIME_ZONE)\\n\\n\\n# This function exists for consistency with get_current_timezone_name\\ndef get_default_timezone_name():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Return the name of the default time zone.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    return _get_timezone_name(get_default_timezone())\\n\\n\\n_active = Local()\\n\\n\\ndef get_current_timezone():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Return the currently active time zone as a tzinfo instance.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    return getattr(_active, \\\"value\\\", get_default_timezone())\\n\\n\\ndef get_current_timezone_name():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Return the name of the currently active time zone.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    return _get_timezone_name(get_current_timezone())\\n\\n\\ndef _get_timezone_name(timezone):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Return the offset for fixed offset timezones, or the name of timezone if\\n    not set.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    return timezone.tzname(None) or str(timezone)\\n\\n# Timezone selection functions.\\n\\n# These functions don't change os.environ['TZ'] and call time.tzset()\\n# because it isn't thread safe.\\n\\n\\ndef activate(timezone):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Set the time zone for the current thread.\\n\\n    The ``timezone`` argument must be an instance of a tzinfo subclass or a\\n    time zone name.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    if isinstance(timezone, tzinfo):\\n        _active.value = timezone\\n    elif isinstance(timezone, str):\\n        if pytz is not None and getattr(settings, 'USE_DEPRECATED_PYTZ', False):\\n            _active.value = pytz.timezone(timezone)\\n        else:\\n            _active.value = zoneinfo.ZoneInfo(timezone)\\n    else:\\n        raise ValueError(\\\"Invalid timezone: %r\\\" % timezone)\\n\\n\\ndef deactivate():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Unset the time zone for the current thread.\\n\\n    Django will then use the time zone defined by settings.TIME_ZONE.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    if hasattr(_active, \\\"value\\\"):\\n        del _active.value\\n\\n\\nclass override(ContextDecorator):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Temporarily set the time zone for the current thread.\\n\\n    This is a context manager that uses django.utils.timezone.activate()\\n    to set the timezone on entry and restores the previously active timezone\\n    on exit.\\n\\n    The ``timezone`` argument must be an instance of a ``tzinfo`` subclass, a\\n    time zone name, or ``None``. If it is ``None``, Django enables the default\\n    time zone.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    def __init__(self, timezone):\\n        self.timezone = timezone\\n\\n    def __enter__(self):\\n        self.old_timezone = getattr(_active, 'value', None)\\n        if self.timezone is None:\\n            deactivate()\\n        else:\\n            activate(self.timezone)\\n\\n    def __exit__(self, exc_type, exc_value, traceback):\\n        if self.old_timezone is None:\\n            deactivate()\\n        else:\\n            _active.value = self.old_timezone\\n\\n\\n# Templates\\n\\ndef template_localtime(value, use_tz=None):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Check if value is a datetime and converts it to local time if necessary.\\n\\n    If use_tz is provided and is not None, that will force the value to\\n    be converted (or not), overriding the value of settings.USE_TZ.\\n\\n    This function is designed for use by the template engine.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    should_convert = (\\n        isinstance(value, datetime) and\\n        (settings.USE_TZ if use_tz is None else use_tz) and\\n        not is_naive(value) and\\n        getattr(value, 'convert_to_local_time', True)\\n    )\\n    return localtime(value) if should_convert else value\\n\\n\\n# Utilities\\n\\ndef localtime(value=None, timezone=None):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Convert an aware datetime.datetime to local time.\\n\\n    Only aware datetimes are allowed. When value is omitted, it defaults to\\n    now().\\n\\n    Local time is defined by the current time zone, unless another time zone\\n    is specified.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    if value is None:\\n        value = now()\\n    if timezone is None:\\n        timezone = get_current_timezone()\\n    # Emulate the behavior of astimezone() on Python < 3.6.\\n    if is_naive(value):\\n        raise ValueError(\\\"localtime() cannot be applied to a naive datetime\\\")\\n    return value.astimezone(timezone)\\n\\n\\ndef localdate(value=None, timezone=None):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Convert an aware datetime to local time and return the value's date.\\n\\n    Only aware datetimes are allowed. When value is omitted, it defaults to\\n    now().\\n\\n    Local time is defined by the current time zone, unless another time zone is\\n    specified.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    return localtime(value, timezone).date()\\n\\n\\ndef now():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Return an aware or naive datetime.datetime, depending on settings.USE_TZ.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    return datetime.now(tz=utc if settings.USE_TZ else None)\\n\\n\\n# By design, these four functions don't perform any checks on their arguments.\\n# The caller should ensure that they don't receive an invalid value like None.\\n\\ndef is_aware(value):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Determine if a given datetime.datetime is aware.\\n\\n    The concept is defined in Python's docs:\\n    https://docs.python.org/library/datetime.html#datetime.tzinfo\\n\\n    Assuming value.tzinfo is either None or a proper datetime.tzinfo,\\n    value.utcoffset() implements the appropriate logic.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    return value.utcoffset() is not None\\n\\n\\ndef is_naive(value):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Determine if a given datetime.datetime is naive.\\n\\n    The concept is defined in Python's docs:\\n    https://docs.python.org/library/datetime.html#datetime.tzinfo\\n\\n    Assuming value.tzinfo is either None or a proper datetime.tzinfo,\\n    value.utcoffset() implements the appropriate logic.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    return value.utcoffset() is None\\n\\n\\n# Sentinel object to detect when is_dst is explicitly passed\\n_is_dst_sentinel = object()\\n\\n\\ndef make_aware(value, timezone=None, is_dst=_is_dst_sentinel):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Make a naive datetime.datetime in a given time zone aware.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    if timezone is None:\\n        timezone = get_current_timezone()\\n    \\n    # Emit deprecation warning if is_dst is explicitly used\\n    if is_dst is not _is_dst_sentinel:\\n        warnings.warn(\\n            \\\"The is_dst argument to make_aware() is deprecated. Use pytz.timezone() \\\"\\n            \\\"with pytz timezones and USE_DEPRECATED_PYTZ=True instead.\\\",\\n            RemovedInDjango50Warning,\\n            stacklevel=2,\\n        )\\n    else:\\n        is_dst = None\\n    \\n    if _is_pytz_zone(timezone):\\n        # This method is available for pytz time zones.\\n        return timezone.localize(value, is_dst=is_dst)\\n    else:\\n        # Check that we won't overwrite the timezone of an aware datetime.\\n        if is_aware(value):\\n            raise ValueError(\\n                \\\"make_aware expects a naive datetime, got %s\\\" % value)\\n        # For zoneinfo timezones, use replace() instead of localize\\n        return value.replace(tzinfo=timezone)\\n\\n\\ndef make_naive(value, timezone=None):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Make an aware datetime.datetime naive in a given time zone.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    if timezone is None:\\n        timezone = get_current_timezone()\\n    # Emulate the behavior of astimezone() on Python < 3.6.\\n    if is_naive(value):\\n        raise ValueError(\\\"make_naive() cannot be applied to a naive datetime\\\")\\n    return value.astimezone(timezone).replace(tzinfo=None)\\n\\n\\ndef _is_pytz_zone(tz):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Checks if a zone is a pytz zone.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    if not _PYTZ_BASE_CLASSES:\\n        return False\\n    return isinstance(tz, _PYTZ_BASE_CLASSES)\\n\\n\\ndef _datetime_ambiguous_or_imaginary(dt, tz):\\n    if _is_pytz_zone(tz):\\n        try:\\n            tz.utcoffset(dt)\\n        except (pytz.AmbiguousTimeError, pytz.NonExistentTimeError):\\n            return True\\n        else:\\n            return False\\n\\n    # For zoneinfo and other timezones, check using fold attribute\\n    return tz.utcoffset(dt.replace(fold=not dt.fold)) != tz.utcoffset(dt)\\n\"}"},"extras":null}}}}}

The zoneinfo implementation is also available\\nvia the backports module `backports.zoneinfo` on Python 3.6+.\\n\\npytz is now deprecated and will be removed in Django 5.0. During the\\ntransitional period, you can set :setting:`USE_DEPRECATED_PYTZ` to ``True``\\nto continue using pytz as the timezone implementation. self.assertIs(ICT, timezone.get_current_timezone())\\n\\n            timezone.deactivate()\\n\\n            func_tz_eat()\\n            self.assertIs(default, timezone.get_current_timezone())\\n\\n {"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"SubagentEvent","payload":{"parent_tool_call_id":"functions.Agent:16","agent_id":"a4a9ace54","subagent_type":"coder","event":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.ReadFile:15","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"   575\t   naive datetime. This indicates that you haven't finished porting your code\n   576\t   for time zone support. Please refer to the :ref:`migration guide\n   577\t   <time-zones-migration-guide>` for tips on this process.\n   578\t\n   579\t   In the meantime, for backwards compatibility, the datetime is considered to\n   580\t   be in the default time zone, which is generally what you expect.\n   581\t\n   582\t#. ``now.date()`` **is yesterday! (or tomorrow)**\n   583\t\n   584\t   If you've always used naive datetimes, you probably believe that you can\n   585\t   convert a datetime to a date by calling its :meth:`~datetime.datetime.date`\n   586\t   method. You also consider that a :class:`~datetime.date` is a lot like a\n   587\t   :class:`~datetime.datetime`, except that it's less accurate.\n   588\t\n   589\t   None of this is true in a time zone aware environment::\n   590\t\n   591\t       >>> import datetime\n   592\t       >>> import pytz\n   593\t       >>> paris_tz = pytz.timezone(\"Europe/Paris\")\n   594\t       >>> new_york_tz = pytz.timezone(\"America/New_York\")\n   595\t       >>> paris = paris_tz.localize(datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 3, 1, 30))\n   596\t       # This is the correct way to convert between time zones with pytz.\n   597\t       >>> new_york = new_york_tz.normalize(paris.astimezone(new_york_tz))\n   598\t       >>> paris == new_york, paris.date() == new_york.date()\n   599\t       (True, False)\n   600\t       >>> paris - new_york, paris.date() - new_york.date()\n   601\t       (datetime.timedelta(0), datetime.timedelta(1))\n   602\t       >>> paris\n   603\t       datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 3, 1, 30, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Europe/Paris' CET+1:00:00 STD>)\n   604\t       >>> new_york\n   605\t       datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 2, 19, 30, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'America/New_York' EST-1 day, 19:00:00 STD>)\n   606\t\n   607\t   As this example shows, the same datetime has a different date, depending on\n   608\t   the time zone in which it is represented. But the real problem is more\n   609\t   fundamental.\n   610\t\n   611\t   A datetime represents a **point in time**. It's absolute: it doesn't depend\n   612\t   on anything. On the contrary, a date is a **calendaring concept**. It's a\n   613\t   period of time whose bounds depend on the time zone in which the date is\n   614\t   considered. As you can see, these two concepts are fundamentally different,\n   615\t   and converting a datetime to a date isn't a deterministic operation.\n   616\t\n   617\t   What does this mean in practice?\n   618\t\n   619\t   Generally, you should avoid converting a :class:`~datetime.datetime` to\n   620\t   :class:`~datetime.date`. For instance, you can use the :tfilter:`date`\n   621\t   template filter to only show the date part of a datetime. This filter will\n   622\t   convert the datetime into the current time zone before formatting it,\n   623\t   ensuring the results appear correctly.\n   624\t\n   625\t   If you really need to do the conversion yourself, you must ensure the\n   626\t   datetime is converted to the appropriate time zone first. Usually, this\n   627\t   will be the current timezone::\n   628\t\n   629\t       >>> from django.utils import timezone\n   630\t       >>> timezone.activate(pytz.timezone(\"Asia/Singapore\"))\n   631\t       # For this example, we set the time zone to Singapore, but here's how\n   632\t       # you would obtain the current time zone in the general case.\n   633\t       >>> current_tz = timezone.get_current_timezone()\n   634\t       # Again, this is the correct way to convert between time zones with pytz.\n   635\t       >>> local = current_tz.normalize(paris.astimezone(current_tz))\n   636\t       >>> local\n   637\t       datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 3, 8, 30, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Asia/Singapore' SGT+8:00:00 STD>)\n   638\t       >>> local.date()\n   639\t       datetime.date(2012, 3, 3)\n   640\t\n   641\t#. **I get an error** \"``Are time zone definitions for your database\n   642\t   installed?``\"\n   643\t\n   644\t   If you are using MySQL, see the :ref:`mysql-time-zone-definitions` section\n   645\t   of the MySQL notes for instructions on loading time zone definitions.\n   646\t\n   647\tUsage\n   648\t-----\n   649\t\n   650\t#. **I have a string** ``\"2012-02-21 10:28:45\"`` **and I know it's in the**\n   651\t   ``\"Europe/Helsinki\"`` **time zone. How do I turn that into an aware\n   652\t   datetime?**\n   653\t\n   654\t   This is exactly what pytz_ is for.\n   655\t\n   656\t       >>> from django.utils.dateparse import parse_datetime\n   657\t       >>> naive = parse_datetime(\"2012-02-21 10:28:45\")\n   658\t       >>> import pytz\n   659\t       >>> pytz.timezone(\"Europe/Helsinki\").localize(naive, is_dst=None)\n   660\t       datetime.datetime(2012, 2, 21, 10, 28, 45, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Europe/Helsinki' EET+2:00:00 STD>)\n   661\t\n   662\t   Note that ``localize`` is a pytz extension to the :class:`~datetime.tzinfo`\n   663\t   API. Also, you may want to catch ``pytz.InvalidTimeError``. The\n   664\t   documentation of pytz contains `more examples`_. You should review it\n   665\t   before attempting to manipulate aware datetimes.\n   666\t\n   667\t#. **How can I obtain the local time in the current time zone?**\n   668\t\n   669\t   Well, the first question is, do you really need to?\n   670\t\n   671\t   You should only use local time when you're interacting with humans, and the\n   672\t   template layer provides :ref:`filters and tags <time-zones-in-templates>`\n   673\t   to convert datetimes to the time zone of your choice.\n   674\t\n   675\t   Furthermore, Python knows how to compare aware datetimes, taking into\n   676\t   account UTC offsets when necessary. It's much easier (and possibly faster)\n   677\t   to write all your model and view code in UTC. So, in most circumstances,\n   678\t   the datetime in UTC returned by :func:`django.utils.timezone.now` will be\n   679\t   sufficient.\n   680\t\n   681\t   For the sake of completeness, though, if you really want the local time\n   682\t   in the current time zone, here's how you can obtain it::\n   683\t\n   684\t       >>> from django.utils import timezone\n   685\t       >>> timezone.localtime(timezone.now())\n   686\t       datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 3, 20, 10, 53, 873365, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Europe/Paris' CET+1:00:00 STD>)\n   687\t\n   688\t   In this example, the current time zone is ``\"Europe/Paris\"``.\n   689\t\n   690\t#. **How can I see all available time zones?**\n   691\t\n   692\t   pytz_ provides helpers_, including a list of current time zones and a list\n   693\t   of all available time zones -- some of which are only of historical\n   694\t   interest. :mod:`zoneinfo` also provides similar functionality via\n","message":"120 lines read from file starting from line 575. If ``timezone`` is set to ``None``, it\\n    defaults to the :ref:`current time zone <default-current-time-zone>`.\\n\\n    When using ``pytz``, the ``pytz.AmbiguousTimeError`` exception is raised if\\n    you try to make ``value`` aware during a DST transition where the same time\\n    occurs twice (when reverting from DST). Setting ``is_dst`` to ``True`` or\\n    ``False`` will avoid the exception by choosing if the time is\\n    pre-transition or post-transition respectively.\\n\\n    When using ``pytz``, the ``pytz.NonExistentTimeError`` exception is raised\\n    if you try to make ``value`` aware during a DST transition such that the\\n    time never occurred. For example, if the 2:00 hour is skipped during a DST\\n    transition, trying to make 2:30 aware in that time zone will raise an\\n    exception. To avoid that you can use ``is_dst`` to specify how\\n    ``make_aware()`` should interpret such a nonexistent time. If\\n    ``is_dst=True`` then the above time would be interpreted as 2:30 DST time\\n    (equivalent to 1:30 local time). Conversely, if ``is_dst=False`` the time\\n    would be interpreted as 2:30 standard time (equivalent to 3:30 local time).\\n\\n    The ``is_dst`` parameter has no effect when using non-``pytz`` timezone\\n    implementations.\", \"new\": \".. function:: make_aware(value, timezone=None, is_dst=None)\\n\\n    Returns an aware :class:`~datetime.datetime` that represents the same\\n    point in time as ``value`` in ``timezone``, ``value`` being a naive\\n    :class:`~datetime.datetime`. If ``timezone`` is set to ``None``, it\\n    defaults to the :ref:`current time zone <default-current-time-zone>`.\\n\\n    When using ``pytz``, the ``pytz.AmbiguousTimeError`` exception is raised if\\n    you try to make ``value`` aware during a DST transition where the same time\\n    occurs twice (when reverting from DST). Setting ``is_dst`` to ``True`` or\\n    ``False`` will avoid the exception by choosing if the time is\\n    pre-transition or post-transition respectively.\\n\\n    When using ``pytz``, the ``pytz.NonExistentTimeError`` exception is raised\\n    if you try to make ``value`` aware during a DST transition such that the\\n    time never occurred. For example, if the 2:00 hour is skipped during a DST\\n    transition, trying to make 2:30 aware in that time zone will raise an\\n    exception. To avoid that you can use ``is_dst`` to specify how\\n    ``make_aware()`` should interpret such a nonexistent time. If\\n    ``is_dst=True`` then the above time would be interpreted as 2:30 DST time\\n    (equivalent to 1:30 local time). Conversely, if ``is_dst=False`` the time\\n    would be interpreted as 2:30 standard time (equivalent to 3:30 local time).\\n\\n    The ``is_dst`` parameter has no effect when using non-``pytz`` timezone\\n    implementations.\\n\\n    .. deprecated:: 4.0\\n\\n        The ``is_dst`` parameter is deprecated and will be removed in Django 5.0.\\n        When using the default :mod:`zoneinfo` implementation, datetime objects\\n        during DST transitions use the ``fold`` attribute to disambiguate instead.\"}}"},"extras":null}}}}}

For example, you can use ``Trunc`` to calculate\\nthe number of sales per day.\\n\\n``Trunc`` takes a single ``expression``, representing a ``DateField``,\\n``TimeField``, or ``DateTimeField``, a ``kind`` representing a date or time\\npart, and an ``output_field`` that's either ``DateTimeField()``,\\n``TimeField()``, or ``DateField()``. It returns a datetime, date, or time\\ndepending on ``output_field``, with fields up to ``kind`` set to their minimum\\nvalue. If ``output_field`` is omitted, it will default to the ``output_field``\\nof ``expression``. A ``tzinfo`` subclass, usually provided by ``pytz``, can be\\npassed to truncate a value in a specific timezone.\\n\\nThe ``is_dst`` parameter indicates whether or not ``pytz`` should interpret\\nnonexistent and ambiguous datetimes in daylight saving time. By default (when\\n``is_dst=None``), ``pytz`` raises an exception for such datetimes.\", \"new\": \".. class:: Trunc(expression, kind, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\\n\\nTruncates a date up to a significant component.\\n\\nWhen you only care if something happened in a particular year, hour, or day,\\nbut not the exact second, then ``Trunc`` (and its subclasses) can be useful to\\nfilter or aggregate your data. For example, you can use ``Trunc`` to calculate\\nthe number of sales per day.\\n\\n``Trunc`` takes a single ``expression``, representing a ``DateField``,\\n``TimeField``, or ``DateTimeField``, a ``kind`` representing a date or time\\npart, and an ``output_field`` that's either ``DateTimeField()``,\\n``TimeField()``, or ``DateField()``. It returns a datetime, date, or time\\ndepending on ``output_field``, with fields up to ``kind`` set to their minimum\\nvalue. If ``output_field`` is omitted, it will default to the ``output_field``\\nof ``expression``. A ``tzinfo`` subclass can be passed to truncate a value in a\\nspecific timezone.\\n\\n.. deprecated:: 4.0\\n\\n    The ``is_dst`` parameter is deprecated and will be removed in Django 5.0.\\n    It was specifically designed for the pytz timezone implementation and has\\n    no effect when using :mod:`zoneinfo` (the default timezone implementation).\\n    Instead of ``is_dst``, the ``fold`` attribute of :class:`datetime.datetime`\\n    objects is used to disambiguate datetimes during DST transitions when using\\n    zoneinfo.\"}, {\"old\": \"``DateField`` truncation\\n~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\\n\\n.. class:: TruncYear(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\\n\\n    .. attribute:: kind = 'year'\\n\\n.. class:: TruncMonth(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\\n\\n    .. attribute:: kind = 'month'\\n\\n.. class:: TruncWeek(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\\n\\n    Truncates to midnight on the Monday of the week.\\n\\n    .. attribute:: kind = 'week'\\n\\n.. class:: TruncQuarter(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\\n\\n    .. attribute:: kind = 'quarter'\", \"new\": \"``DateField`` truncation\\n~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\\n\\n.. class:: TruncYear(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\\n\\n    .. attribute:: kind = 'year'\\n\\n    .. deprecated:: 4.0\\n\\n        The ``is_dst`` parameter is deprecated. The example below converts to\n   450\tthe Melbourne timezone (UTC +10:00), which changes the day, weekday, and hour\n   451\tvalues that are returned::\n   452\t\n   453\t    >>> import pytz\n   454\t    >>> melb = pytz.timezone('Australia/Melbourne')  # UTC+10:00\n   455\t    >>> with timezone.override(melb):\n   456\t    ... If a different timezone is active in Django, the datetime is converted\\nto that timezone before the value is extracted. The example below converts to\\nthe Melbourne timezone (UTC +10:00), which changes the day, weekday, and hour\\nvalues that are returned::\\n\\n    >>> from zoneinfo import ZoneInfo\\n    >>> melb = ZoneInfo('Australia/Melbourne')  # UTC+10:00\\n    >>> with timezone.override(melb):\\n    ... Each :class:`datetime.datetime`\n   821\tobject in the result list is \"truncated\" to the given ``type``.\n   822\t\n   823\t``order``, which defaults to ``'ASC'``, should be either ``'ASC'`` or\n   824\t``'DESC'``. This specifies how to order the results.\n   825\t\n   826\t``tzinfo`` defines the time zone to which datetimes are converted prior to\n   827\ttruncation. Indeed, a given datetime has different representations depending\n   828\ton the time zone in use. This parameter must be a :class:`datetime.tzinfo`\n   829\tobject. If it's ``None``, Django uses the :ref:`current time zone\n   830\t<default-current-time-zone>`. It has no effect when :setting:`USE_TZ` is\n   831\t``False``.\n   832\t\n   833\t``is_dst`` indicates whether or not ``pytz`` should interpret nonexistent and\n   834\tambiguous datetimes in daylight saving time. By default (when ``is_dst=None``),\n   835\t``pytz`` raises an exception for such datetimes.\n   836\t\n   837\t.. _database-time-zone-definitions:\n   838\t\n   839\t.. note::\n   840\t\n   841\t    This function performs time zone conversions directly in the database.\n   842\t    As a consequence, your database must be able to interpret the value of\n   843\t    ``tzinfo.tzname(None)``. This translates into the following requirements:\n   844\t\n   845\t    - SQLite: no requirements. Conversions are performed in Python with pytz_\n   846\t      (installed when you install Django).\n   847\t    - PostgreSQL: no requirements (see `Time Zones`_).\n   848\t    - Oracle: no requirements (see `Choosing a Time Zone File`_).\n   849\t    - MySQL: load the time zone tables with `mysql_tzinfo_to_sql`_.\n   850\t\n   851\t    .. _pytz: http://pytz.sourceforge.net/\n   852\t    .. _Time Zones: https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/datatype-datetime.html#DATATYPE-TIMEZONES\n   853\t    .. _Choosing a Time Zone File: https://docs.oracle.com/en/database/oracle/\n   854\t       oracle-database/18/nlspg/datetime-data-types-and-time-zone-support.html\n   855\t       #GUID-805AB986-DE12-4FEA-AF56-5AABCD2132DF\n   856\t    .. _mysql_tzinfo_to_sql: https://dev.mysql.com/doc/refman/en/mysql-tzinfo-to-sql.html\n   857\t\n   858\t``none()``\n   859\t~~~~~~~~~~\n","message":"60 lines read from file starting from line 800.        [datetime.datetime(2011, 1, 1, 1, 30, 0, tzinfo=EAT),\\n             datetime.datetime(2011, 1, 1, 4, 30, 0, tzinfo=EAT)]\\n        )\\n\\n    @skipUnlessDBFeature('has_zoneinfo_database')\\n self.assertIsNone(e.dt)\\n\\n    def test_update_with_timedelta(self):\\n        initial_dt = timezone.now().replace(microsecond=0)\\n        event = Event.objects.create(dt=initial_dt)\\n timedelta(hours=2))\\n\\n\\n@override_settings(TIME_ZONE='Africa/Nairobi', USE_TZ=True)\\nclass ForcedTimeZoneDatabaseTests(TransactionTestCase):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Test the TIME_ZONE database configuration parameter.\\n\\n    Since this involves reading and writing to the same database through two\\n    connections, this is a TransactionTestCase.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n\\n    available_apps = ['timezones']\\n\\n    @classmethod\\n    def setUpClass(cls):\\n        # @skipIfDBFeature and @skipUnlessDBFeature cannot be chained. The\\n        # outermost takes precedence. Handle skipping manually instead.\\n        if connection.features.supports_timezones:\\n            raise SkipTest(\\\"Database has feature(s) supports_timezones\\\")\\n        if not connection.features.test_db_allows_multiple_connections:\\n            raise SkipTest(\\\"Database doesn't support feature(s): test_db_allows_multiple_connections\\\")\\n\\n        super().setUpClass()\\n\\n    def test_read_datetime(self):\\n        fake_dt = datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 17, 20, 30, tzinfo=UTC)\\n        Event.objects.create(dt=fake_dt)\\n\\n        with override_database_connection_timezone('Asia/Bangkok'):\\n            event = Event.objects.get()\\n            dt = datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 10, 20, 30, tzinfo=UTC)\\n        self.assertEqual(event.dt, dt)\\n\\n    def test_write_datetime(self):\\n        dt = datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 10, 20, 30, tzinfo=UTC)\\n        with override_database_connection_timezone('Asia/Bangkok'):\\n            Event.objects.create(dt=dt)\\n\\n        event = Event.objects.get()\\n        fake_dt = datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 17, 20, 30, tzinfo=UTC)\\n        self.assertEqual(event.dt, fake_dt)\\n\\n\\n@override_settings(TIME_ZONE='Africa/Nairobi')\\nclass SerializationTests(SimpleTestCase):\\n\\n    # Backend-specific notes:\\n    # - JSON supports only milliseconds, microseconds will be truncated.\\n    # - PyYAML dumps the UTC offset correctly for timezone-aware datetimes.\\n    #   When PyYAML < 5.3 loads this representation, it subtracts the offset\\n    #   and returns a naive datetime object in UTC. PyYAML 5.3+ loads timezones\\n    #   correctly.\\n    # Tests are adapted to take these quirks into account.\\n\\n    def assert_python_contains_datetime(self, objects, dt):\\n        self.assertEqual(objects[0]['fields']['dt'], dt)\\n\\n    def assert_json_contains_datetime(self, json, dt):\\n        self.assertIn('\\\"fields\\\": {\\\"dt\\\": \\\"%s\\\"}' % dt, json)\\n\\n    def assert_xml_contains_datetime(self, xml, dt):\\n        field = parseString(xml).getElementsByTagName('field')[0]\\n        self.assertXMLEqual(field.childNodes[0].wholeText, dt)\\n\\n    def assert_yaml_contains_datetime(self, yaml, dt):\\n        # Depending on the yaml dumper, '!timestamp' might be absent\\n        self.assertRegex(yaml, r\\\"\\\\n  fields: {dt: !(!timestamp)? '%s'}\\\" % re.escape(dt))\\n\\n    def test_naive_datetime(self):\\n        dt = datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 13, 20, 30)\\n\\n        data = serializers.serialize('python', [Event(dt=dt)])\\n        self.assert_python_contains_datetime(data, dt)\\n        obj = next(serializers.deserialize('python', data)).object\\n        self.assertEqual(obj.dt, dt)\\n\\n        data = serializers.serialize('json', [Event(dt=dt)])\\n        self.assert_json_contains_datetime(data, \\\"2011-09-01T13:20:30\\\")\\n        obj = next(serializers.deserialize('json', data)).object\\n        self.assertEqual(obj.dt, dt)\\n\\n        data = serializers.serialize('xml', [Event(dt=dt)])\\n        self.assert_xml_contains_datetime(data, \\\"2011-09-01T13:20:30\\\")\\n        obj = next(serializers.deserialize('xml', data)).object\\n        self.assertEqual(obj.dt, dt)\\n\\n        if not isinstance(serializers.get_serializer('yaml'), serializers.BadSerializer):\\n            data = serializers.serialize('yaml', [Event(dt=dt)], default_flow_style=None)\\n            self.assert_yaml_contains_datetime(data, \\\"2011-09-01 13:20:30\\\")\\n            obj = next(serializers.deserialize('yaml', data)).object\\n            self.assertEqual(obj.dt, dt)\\n\\n    def test_naive_datetime_with_microsecond(self):\\n        dt = datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 13, 20, 30, 405060)\\n\\n        data = serializers.serialize('python', [Event(dt=dt)])\\n        self.assert_python_contains_datetime(data, dt)\\n        obj = next(serializers.deserialize('python', data)).object\\n        self.assertEqual(obj.dt, dt)\\n\\n        data = serializers.serialize('json', [Event(dt=dt)])\\n        self.assert_json_contains_datetime(data, \\\"2011-09-01T13:20:30.405\\\")\\n        obj = next(serializers.deserialize('json', data)).object\\n        self.assertEqual(obj.dt.replace(microsecond=405000))\\n\\n        data = serializers.serialize('xml', [Event(dt=dt)])\\n        self.assert_xml_contains_datetime(data, \\\"2011-09-01T13:20:30.405060\\\")\\n        obj = next(serializers.deserialize('xml', data)).object\\n        self.assertEqual(obj.dt, dt)\\n\\n        if not isinstance(serializers.get_serializer('yaml'), serializers.BadSerializer):\\n            data = serializers.serialize('yaml', [Event(dt=dt)], default_flow_style=None)\\n            self.assert_yaml_contains_datetime(data, \\\"2011-09-01 13:20:30.405060\\\")\\n            obj = next(serializers.deserialize('yaml', data)).object\\n            self.assertEqual(obj.dt, dt)\\n\\n    def test_aware_datetime_with_microsecond(self):\\n        dt = datetime.datetime(2011, 9, 1, 17, 20, 30, 405060, tzinfo=ICT)\\n\\n        data = self.assertEqual(obj.dt, dt)\\n\\n        data = serializers.serialize('json', [Event(dt=dt)])\\n        self.assert_json_contains_datetime(data, \\\"2011-09-01T17:20:30.405+07:00\\\")\\n        obj = next(serializers.deserialize('json', data)).object\\n        self.assertEqual(obj.dt.replace(microsecond=405000))\\n\\n        data = serializers.serialize('xml', [Event(dt=dt)])\\n        self.assert_xml_contains_datetime(data, \\\"2011-09-01T17:20:30.405060+07:00\\\")\\n        obj = next(serializers.deserialize('xml', data)).object\\n        self.assertEqual(obj.dt, dt)\\n\\n        if not isinstance(serializers.get_serializer('yaml'), serializers.BadSerializer):\\n            data = serializers.serialize('yaml', [Event(dt=dt)], default_flow_style=None)\\n            self.assert_yaml_contains_datetime(data, \\\"2011-09-01 17:20:30.405060+07:00\\\")\\n            obj = next(serializers.deserialize('yaml', data)).object\\n            if HAS_YAML and yaml.__version__ < '5.3':\\n                self.assertEqual(obj.dt.replace(tzinfo=UTC), dt)\\n            else:\\n                self.assertEqual(obj.dt, dt)\\n\\n    def test_aware_datetime_in_utc(self):\\n
